channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Artemis II Completes First Day of Its NASA Lunar Mission
item.description: The crew, three Americans and a Canadian, are the first humans to travel to the moon in more than 50 years. They will not land on the surface, but the mission will pave the way for future visits.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times

item.title: China’s Aiming for the Moon, and NASA Is Looking Over Its Shoulder
item.description: The U.S. space agency launched a lunar flyby Wednesday, but Beijing is pursuing its own space program with formidable focus. Here’s what we know about it, in photos and videos.
item.dc:creator: Selam Gebrekidan and Joy Dong

item.title: The Artemis II Toilet Had a Problem
item.description: The Universal Waste Management System aboard the Orion capsule is an innovation in deep space toiletry (it seems to be fixed now).
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Orion training module, an exact replica of the spacecraft that will take astronauts around the moon, at the Johnson Space Center in Houston. A compartment in the floor houses the only bathroom on board.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From Trump’s Address on Iran
item.description: President Trump did not define a clear path out of the conflict, which he estimated would end within three weeks.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Alex Brandon
item.media:description: President Trump giving a prime-time address from the White House on Wednesday.

item.title: Iran War Showcases Strength of South Korean Defense Sector
item.description: Missile interceptors made by the South Korean firm LIG Nex1 are said to be performing well, at a small fraction of the cost of U.S. interceptors.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik and Farah Stockman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:credit: Chris Jung/NurPhoto, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Cheongung-II, a South Korean air defense system, on display last year. The country’s defense companies are rapidly taking on new business, as U.S. firms struggle to keep pace with demand from multiple conflicts.

item.title: Trump Initially Laid Out Five Goals for the Iran War. Here’s Where They Stand.
item.description: The United States and Israel have done significant damage to Iran’s military capabilities. But Iran still fires missiles, has nuclear material and coordinates with militias in the region.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A new mural in Valiasr Square in Tehran on Wednesday. A large number of Iran’s ballistic missiles and launchers remain undamaged after a month of airstrikes by the United States and Israel.

item.title: Senate and House Republicans Strike Deal to End DHS Shutdown
item.description: A bill to reopen the department, which the House G.O.P. rejected on Friday, could be approved as early as Thursday. It was a sharp turnaround by the lawmakers and President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: The resurrected plan would omit money for Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol, the agencies carrying out President Trump’s immigration crackdown. Republicans said those agencies’ employees would continue to be paid out of funds that were pushed through Congress last year.

item.title: Trump Has Discussed Firing Attorney General Pam Bondi
item.description: President Trump has not made a final decision, but he has floated the idea of replacing Ms. Bondi with Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Among President Trump’s top complaints about Attorney General Pam Bondi is her handling of the Epstein files, which has become a political liability for the president among his supporters.

item.title: Bondi Is Vulnerable as Republican Frustrations Over DOJ’s Epstein Files Missteps Grow
item.description: Ms. Bondi’s critics inside and outside the administration say she has made unforced errors that have turned the Justice Department’s handling of the Epstein files into a political crisis.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: Key Justices Appear Skeptical of Limiting Birthright Citizenship
item.description: President Trump appeared in court, watching as members of the court’s conservative majority raised questions about his efforts to limit birthright citizenship.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of birthright citizenship gathered outside the Supreme Court during oral arguments on Wednesday.

item.title: Five Takeaways From the Supreme Court’s Birthright Citizenship Case
item.description: The justices grappled with questions about domiciles and foundlings, avoided policy debates and mused about the sweep of possible rulings.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak and Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The case argued on Wednesday focused on the constitutionality of an executive order seeking to end citizenship for babies born on U.S. soil to undocumented immigrants and temporary foreign visitors.

item.title: Trump Attends Supreme Court Oral Arguments, Then Leaves an Hour In
item.description: President Trump’s presence in the court, a presidential first, put him face to face with justices whom he has tried to bully and intimidate.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow, Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Chief Justice John G. Roberts Jr. and Justices Elena Kagan, Brett M. Kavanaugh and Amy Coney Barrett attended President Trump’s State of the Union address in February.

item.title: Immigrant Families Are Cautiously Hopeful Over Supreme Court Birthright Citizenship Case
item.description: As the high court heard arguments on whether to limit birthright citizenship, many people pondered what the decision could mean for future generations.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan and Zach Montague
item.media:credit: via the Andreote family
item.media:description: Maria and Francisco Andreote are Brazilian microbiologists conducting research at Penn State. Their daughter, Laura, 4, was born in the United States just before the birthright citizenship case landed in the Supreme Court.

item.title: Tech Bros Hacked Their Diets. Now You May Be Doing It, Too.
item.description: With seeds, supplements and gadgets (but little expert guidance), Americans of all stripes are seeking wellness through what they eat.
item.dc:creator: Priya Krishna
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kyle Taylor, a college student, wears a smart watch that tracks various health metrics via an app.

item.title: Ocasio-Cortez Says She Will Oppose All U.S. Military Aid to Israel
item.description: Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez of New York said she would oppose U.S. military aid to Israel, including for defensive systems.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk, Benjamin Oreskes and Kellen Browning
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez has emerged as a leader in the Democratic Party and is considered a potential 2028 presidential candidate.

item.title: Trump-Backed Candidate in Louisiana Primary Also Praised DEI. Will It Hurt Her?
item.description: Representative Julia Letlow of Louisiana, running to challenge Senator Bill Cassidy, is facing conservative blowback over remarks from 2020.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Michael Johnson/The Advocate, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Representative Julia Letlow of Louisiana

item.title: Medical Examiner Rules That a Rohingya Refugee’s Death Was a Homicide
item.description: An autopsy showed that the man suffered dehydration and hypothermia after Border Patrol agents dropped him off on a cold night in Buffalo. Gov. Kathy Hochul condemned their actions as cruel and inhumane.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ley
item.media:credit: Craig Ruttle/Reuters
item.media:description: The body of Nurul Amin Shah Alam was placed in a grave in Buffalo in February.

item.title: Judge Finds Border Officials Violated Previous Order on Warrantless Arrests
item.description: A federal judge in California ordered agents to thoroughly document any future stops in an area spanning 34 counties.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A day laborer in Bakersfield, Calif., in January. A federal judge found on Wednesday that border officials appeared to have violated her earlier order when agents carried out an immigration sweep last year in a Home Depot parking lot in Sacramento.

item.title: Goodbye ‘Geeky Hunk’? Gmail Users Can Now Change Their Usernames.
item.description: Users who have been saddled with now-cringe email handles since the mid-2000s can now change them without losing any data under a policy Google announced on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Sunday Alamba/Associated Press
item.media:description: Google announced on Wednesday that Gmail users would have the option to change their email addresses without losing access to their inboxes.

item.title: Syracuse Drops 84 Programs Including Classics, Ceramics and Italian
item.description: In all, 93 of the 460 academic programs at the university will be closed or paused. No students were majoring in 55 of the programs that are ending.
item.dc:creator: Sharon Otterman
item.media:credit: Mike Groll/Associated Press
item.media:description: Even though Syracuse University is eliminating or halting enrollment in 20 percent of the degree programs, coursework in those subjects will continue.

item.title: Lawsuits Are the New Trump Tactic in the Fight to Overhaul Education
item.description: Trump officials have faced dozens of lawsuits over their aggressive efforts to force change in universities and school districts. Now Trump lawyers are taking schools to court.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender and Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: The University of Pennsylvania’s campus in Philadelphia. The Trump administration won in court this week after suing Penn to obtain lists of Jewish staff for an antisemitism investigation.

item.title: DNA Confirms Ted Bundy Killed Utah Teen in 1974, Investigators Say
item.description: Mr. Bundy had confessed to killing Laura Ann Aime before he was executed in 1989. Investigators said DNA testing provided conclusive proof.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson

item.title: What to Know About Ted Bundy, the Notorious Serial Killer
item.description: On Wednesday, the authorities said DNA evidence had linked Mr. Bundy, who was executed in 1989, to the murder of a 17-year-old Utah girl in 1974.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Ted Bundy at the second day of jury selection in his murder trial on June 27, 1979, in Miami.

item.title: The Only Thing Trump Is Missing in Cuba Is a Plan
item.description: Starving Cubans does nothing to advance American interests.
item.dc:creator: Ricardo Zúniga
item.media:credit: Adalberto Roque/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Remember the Oil Shocks of the ’70s? This Is Going to Be Worse. Much Worse.
item.description: Higher oil prices and limited supply will accelerate a turn toward cleaner technologies, especially in Asia and Europe.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Mingle
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Man in Custody After 7-Month-Old Girl Is Fatally Shot in Brooklyn
item.description: The baby was struck by a stray bullet on Wednesday afternoon in the Williamsburg neighborhood. The police said they were still looking for another person.
item.dc:creator: Chelsia Rose Marcius and Nate Schweber
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Officers were investigating the fatal shooting of a 7-month-old girl in Brooklyn on Wednesday afternoon.

item.title: Migrating American Woodcocks Set Off a Birding Frenzy in NYC’s Bryant Park
item.description: American woodcocks, also known as timberdoodles, are bopping and shimmying through the park, charming visitors during a pit stop on their northerly migration.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Neil Vigdor/The New York Times
item.media:description: An American woodcock visited Bryant Park in Manhattan on Tuesday on its way north.

item.title: Texas Man Charged With Manslaughter in Connection With Cold Case
item.description: Prosecutors in Galveston County say that in the 1980s, a man killed a teenager and helped hide the remains of a woman, two of the dozens of bodies found on land known as the Texas Killing Fields.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Buster Dean/Houston Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Tim Miller, father of the victim Laura Miller, hugging his wife, Georgeann, in 1998 as they looked at one of the billboards seeking information to help solve killings in the Houston area.

item.title: Tennessee Library Director Luanne James Fired Over L.G.B.T.Q. Books
item.description: The director, Luanne James, was fired at a board meeting for the Rutherford County Library System on Monday after she refused to move certain books to the adult section.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane and Sonia A. Rao
item.media:credit: Ryan Rehnborn/Rutherford County Library Alliance, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Shortly before being fired, Luanne James said she stood by her decision.
